headlines.title: Girl's death after medical emergency at San Jose home under investigation
headlines.description: Police are investigating the death of an 8-year-old girl who was rushed from a San Jose home to the hospital last week. Officers responded to a home on Lancelot Lane shortly before 10 a.m. Wednesday to reports that a child had a medical emergency, San Jose police said. Information about the nature of the injuries wasn’t released. The child, identified as Aurora Williams, was transported to a hospital, but authorities were notified on Sunday that the youth had died. All major injury or death investigations involving a child are investigated, per Santa Clara County protocol. Police are working with the Santa Clara County District Attorney’s Office on the case. San Jose May 13 San Jose Apr 23 San Jose Apr 23 Meanwhile, the death adds to mounting trouble for the Department of Family and Children Services in Santa Clara County. The agency is facing criticism from the death, which comes on the heels of another child’s death under the watch of the same department last month. A source close to the social services agency told NBC Bay Area the Department of Children and Family Services had responded to Aurora’s home in the past. The Santa Clara Board of Supervisors on Tuesday received an earful from worried employees and community members. Supervisors also on Tuesday received the child death review report for the years 2021 to 2023. “We owe it to that youth, the community, their family, what could we have done? Is there anything we could have done to prevent the death,” Santa Clara County Public Health Officer Dr. Sarah Rudman said. The report shows that preventable child deaths had been decreasing over the previous five years. But it did not address the recent deaths this year of baby Jaxon and now 8-year-old Aurora. In April, baby Jaxon died after allegedly being repeatedly sexually assaulted inside his San Jose foster home. The 2-year-old’s foster brother is facing murder charges. In addition, 10 employees at the Department of Children and Family Services were placed on leave. The Santa Clara County Medical Examiner’s Office on Tuesday said it is still not releasing the manner and cause of Aurora’s death. Police and the district attorney are investigating her death. County supervisors are looking to implement reforms in the child care system in hopes of preventing another tragedy. Bay City News contributed to this report.

headlines.title: Berkeley now requiring home sellers to make eco-friendly upgrades
headlines.description: Berkeley home seller are now required to make eco-friendly upgrades or pay up before handing over the keys. While other cities encourage green improvements, Berkeley is believed to be the first in the nation to tie mandatory upgrades directly to the sale of a home. Velena Jones explains. Watch her report in the video above. Real Estate Apr 28 Berkeley May 8 UC Berkeley Apr 24 Digital detox: UC Berkeley students ditch phones for in-person connection

headlines.title: San Francisco approves loan to keep zoo operating
headlines.description: The San Francisco Board of Supervisors approved an $8.5 million loan on Tuesday to help keep the city’s nearly 100-year-old zoo operating. The loan comes with some strings attached for the beleaguered non-profit that is charged with managing the zoo. Sergio Quintana has more. Watch his report in the video above. San Francisco Nov 20, 2025 San Francisco Jul 11, 2025 San Francisco Feb 7, 2025

headlines.title: San Jose fire response: Crews report blaze now under control
headlines.description: San Jose fire crews responded to three separate small brush fires Tuesday afternoon. The string of fires were reported in the area of Story Road and Remillard Court, just south of Interstate 280. The San Jose Fire Department said traffic in the area is impacted and the public should avoid the area. Emergency crews were first notified about the fires at 12:14 p.m. The fire department reported the blaze was under control at 12:44 p.m. No other information was immediately available. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: BTS fans gear up for final Stanford show
headlines.description: It’s the last night of K-pop royalty in the Bay Area. BTS is set to perform its final Bay Area show Tuesday night at Stanford Stadium, where another sold-out crowd is expected to attend. Fans lined up outside the venue throughout the day, with some camping overnight to buy merchandise before items sold out. “To get merch, it sells out pretty fast,” one fan, Jazlyn, said. She said she arrived at 7 p.m. Monday night and got about 30 minutes of sleep. The K-pop group is wrapping up a trio of sold-out concerts at Stanford, with an estimated 50,000 fans attending each performance. Resale tickets for Tuesday night’s show were still available online, though the cheapest seats were listed for more than $300. Peninsula May 17 synced in May 18 Peninsula May 15 Increased traffic expected as BTS concerts at Stanford Stadium draw big crowds

headlines.title: 6-year-old boy dies at afterschool program in Burlingame
headlines.description: A Peninsula family is mourning the loss of their 6-year-old son who they say was killed when a table fell on him at a Burlingame afterschool program. The child died Wednesday at McKinley Elementary School, according to a letter sent to community parents from Burlingame School District Superintendent Marla Silversmith, who added the victim was not a student in the district. “While we believe this was an isolated incident, it is being thoroughly reviewed through the proper channels to determine the cause,” Silversmith said in the letter to parents. “As the review is ongoing, I am sorry that we are unable to provide additional information at this time. However, out of an abundance of caution, the District immediately took precautionary steps following the accident, including securing and temporarily closing all District MPRs and reviewing and evaluating procedures related to the use and rental of District facilities by third-party organizations.” The San Mateo County Coroner’s Office has identified the boy as Gavin Tsui. Millbrae Elementary School District Superintendent Lisa Hickey in a statement to NBC Bay Area confirmed the student was from the neighboring district. “It is with a heavy heart and profound sadness that we share the devastating news of the passing of one of our students who attended Meadows Elementary,” Hickey said. “The entire Millbrae community is heartbroken by this tragic loss. There are no words to adequately express the depth of our grief, or the sorrow we feel for the student’s family, friends, caring adults, and classmates.” Champions, which runs an afterschool program at McKinley Elementary School, issued a statement saying the boy was not attending their program. It is unclear what program the boy was attending. Parents at the school on Monday said the campus sent them an email offering counseling for students to help them cope with their loss.

headlines.title: Teen was speeding and driving recklessly prior to deadly Oakland crash, police say
headlines.description: Oakland police released new details Monday in a crash that killed three pedestrians and injured three others over the weekend. A teenage driver was going more than 50 miles per hour before he hit six people on International Boulevard late Saturday, according to police. The 17-year-old driver had been speeding and driving recklessly on 85th Avenue, police said, adding just as the driver turned left onto International Boulevard, he hit several parked cars before jumping the curb and striking the victims. Officers said the teen then tried to run, but was stopped by bystanders and arrested by police. Police said one person remains in critical condition, another is listed as stable and a third injured person has been released from the hospital. Oakland May 17 Oakland May 17 Oakland May 14 Boys and Girls Club to open first playground on Oakland's International Boulevard

headlines.title: World Cup tickets in Bay Area among tournament's cheapest
headlines.description: While housing, grocery and gasoline prices continue to climb across the Bay Area, one major expense is dropping ahead of the 2026 FIFA World Cup: soccer tickets. Prices for tickets to the six World Cup matches scheduled next month at Levi’s Stadium in Santa Clara have fallen about 24% over the past month on the secondary market, according to TicketData, a company that tracks ticket sales. FIFA World Cup May 13 FIFA World Cup May 14 FIFA World Cup May 14 The decline means fans can now find tickets to some group-stage matches for as low as $180, with others listed around $275 — prices considered relatively low for the men’s FIFA World Cup. Industry analysts said the lower prices are likely tied to the matchups scheduled for the Bay Area. “I think a lot of it, of course, has to do with the matchups,” said Keith Pagello, founder of TicketData. Levi’s Stadium is set to host games including Qatar vs. Switzerland, Austria vs. Jordan and Paraguay vs. Turkey. NBC Bay Area’s Bob Redell has more in the video above.

headlines.title: San Jose lands professional women's hockey team
headlines.description: The Bay Area is getting a new professional sports team. The Professional Women’s Hockey League announced Tuesday that it is expanding to SAP Center for the upcoming season that opens in December. With the PWHL season starting in a few months, San Jose has got a franchise gearing up. “San Jose and the Bay Area is about dreamers, it’s about believers, it’s about innovators, startup culture and moving forward fast,” Bay FC cofounder Brandi Chastain said during the announcement event. Fans also expressed excitement about the new team’s arrival. “I’m really excited,” fan Melody Ray said. “Me and my auntie go to a lot of the Sharks games and Barracuda games, so I’m really excited to experience this with her too.” Organizers pointed to the success of other Bay Area women’s sports franchises, including the WNBA’s Golden State Valkyries and the National Women’s Soccer League’s Bay FC. “There is that element of, hey, this is a women’s sports hub across the country,” Bay FC cofounder Aly Wagner said. “If we start to own that narrative, there is a lot of value that will flow downstream to our city, to our community.” San Jose is one of the league’s four new expansion franchises, growing the league’s team count to 12. League officials said the team colors will be orange, blue and white, though the franchise has not yet announced a nickname or mascot. Fans can begin placing deposits for season tickets starting Tuesday, while the team begins recruiting players and staff.

headlines.title: Cupertino proposes new pickleball play hours in response to noise complaints
headlines.description: There is a lot of back and forth in a fight over pickleball noise at public park courts in Cupertino. The city is now sounding off, saying it is ready to implement a pilot program in hopes of quieting the complaints. In response to complaints about the noise from its courts at Memorial Park, the city approved a pilot program to limit free drop-in Pickleball play hours. The courts will be open from 9 a.m. to 7 p.m. Tuesday through Sunday, with no pickleball allowed at all on Mondays. The new hours go into effect on July 7 However, pickleball players are clapping back at the proposed pilot program. Robert Handa takes a closer look at how many are hoping to find some middle ground. Watch his report in the video above. San Jose May 5 Martinez Mar 18 San Francisco Sep 23, 2025 San Francisco works to open new pickleball courts without upsetting residents

headlines.title: District attorneys raise concerns about California Supreme Court's cash bail ruling
headlines.description: Two Bay Area district attorneys are raising concerns about a recent California Supreme Court ruling regarding cash bail. San Francisco District Attorney Brooke Jenkins said the decision could threaten public safety in the city, noting the Public Defender’s Office has already filed motions calling for repeat drug offenders and robbery suspects to be released without a cash bond because of the ruling. Sergio Quintana has more in the video above.

headlines.title: OpenAI trial: Jury tosses out Elon Musk's lawsuit against Sam Altman
headlines.description: What to Know Elon Musk filed a $134 billion lawsuit against Sam Altman and his top lieutenant, Greg Brockman, along with Microsoft, over its investments in OpenAI, in 2024. Musk claimed OpenAI violated its original mission to operate as a nonprofit focused on developing artificial intelligence for the benefit of humanity. OpenAI brushed off Musk’s allegations as a case of sour grapes aimed at undercutting its rapid growth and bolstering Musk’s own xAI, which he launched in 2023 as a competitor. The nine-person jury was selected on April 27. The trial was held in Oakland federal court and lasted three weeks. Testimony in the trial ended Wednesday, May 13. Closing arguments in the trial wrapped Thursday, May 14. Jurors began deliberations on Monday, May 18. A federal court on Monday dismissed claims filed against OpenAI and its top executives by Elon Musk, who accused them of betraying a shared vision for it to remain a nonprofit dedicated to guiding artificial intelligence’s development for the good of humanity. Musk, the world’s richest man, was a co-founder of OpenAI, which launched in 2015 and went on to create ChatGPT. After investing $38 million in its first years, Musk accused OpenAI CEO Sam Altman and his top deputy of shifting into a moneymaking mode behind his back. The nine-person jury found that Musk waited too long to file his lawsuit and missed the deadline for the statute of limitations. The jury had deliberated only two hours. The jury served in an advisory role, but Judge Yvonne Gonzalez Rogers accepted the verdict Monday as the court’s own and dismissed Musk’s claims. The three-week trial in Oakland, California shed light on the bitter falling-out between the two Silicon Valley titans and the beginnings of OpenAI, now a company valued at $852 billion and moving toward potentially one of the largest initial public offerings in history. Altman and OpenAI claimed there was never a promise to keep OpenAI a nonprofit forever. In fact, they argued, Musk knew this and filed his lawsuit because he couldn’t have unilateral control over the fast-growing AI developer. Musk was seeking damages to be paid to the altruistic efforts of OpenAI’s charitable arm as well as Altman’s ouster from OpenAI’s board. Musk’s decision to stop funding the company contributed to a bitter rift between the former allies. Musk says he was responding to deceptive conduct that OpenAI’s board picked up on when it fired Altman as CEO in 2023 before he got his job back days later. Musk did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Oakland May 14 Artificial intelligence May 14 Artificial intelligence May 12 The three-week trial saw testimony from Musk, Altman and his top lieutenant Greg Brockman, along with Microsoft CEO Satya Nadella and a slew of others in the tech titans’ orbit. Musk told jurors on his first of three days on the stand that, fundamentally, “I think they’re going to try to make this lawsuit … very complicated, but it’s actually very simple,” Musk said. “Which is that it’s not OK to steal a charity.” Musk’s lawsuit claimed that, in addition to “breach of charitable trust,” Altman and Brockman unjustly enriched themselves from the windfall as the ChatGPT maker soared in valuation. Brockman revealed during the trial that his stake in OpenAI is worth about $30 billion. OpenAI has brushed off Musk’s allegations as an unfounded case of sour grapes aimed at undercutting its rapid growth and bolstering Musk’s own xAI, which he launched in 2023 as a competitor. During cross-examination, Musk was sometimes combative with OpenAI lawyer William Savitt. “Your questions are not simple,” Musk said at one point. “They are designed to trick me essentially.” Jurors also heard from witnesses including OpenAI ex-board members Helen Toner and Tasha McCauley, who spoke about the decision to fire Altman in 2023. They were ousted from the board themselves when Altman returned to his role a few days later. Altman and Musk both vied to be OpenAI’s CEO in its early years. In his testimony, Altman said he had concerns about Musk’s attempts to gain more control over OpenAI, which was aiming to safely build a better-than-human form of AI called artificial general intelligence. “Part of the reason we started OpenAI is we didn’t think AGI could be under the control of any one person, no matter how good their intents are,” Altman said. Near the end of his testimony, Altman said that before things turned sour, he had thought very highly of Musk. “I felt like he had abandoned us, not come through on his promises, put the company in a very difficult place, jeopardized the mission, didn’t really care about the things I thought he cared about,” Altman said. “It’s been an extremely painful thing for me … to have someone that I respected so much not acknowledge that and continue to publicly attack us.”
